methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $800M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Biotech & Health. Range: $600M - $3.3B.

methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Biotech & Health P/S median is 6.1x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Asimov, a Series B biotech company founded by Chris Voigt, Doug Densmore, Alec Nielsen, and Raja Srinivas, is valued at $800M post-money following $209M in total capital raised. The valuation framework draws on a comparable universe of 4 same-stage, same-sector private peers (Eikon Therapeutics, Neko Health, New Limit, Profluent) valued between $600M and $3.3B, plus 3 public biotech benchmarks (RXRX, TEM, ONT.L).

Two triangulation methods are applicable. The stage-progression approach, applying a 1.9x multiplier typical of Series B-to-later-stage progression to the $800M base, yields $1.5B. The peer-median method, anchoring to the $1.3B median valuation across the four comparable private companies at the same stage, suggests $1.3B. Public-comp anchoring via price-to-sales multiples (sector median 6.1x) cannot be applied due to absence of disclosed revenue for the target.

Synthesizing the two applicable methods produces a valuation range of $1.1B (low) to $1.7B (high), with a median of $1.4B. This represents approximately 75% upside from the current $800M post-money valuation. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the four peer comparables—given the wide $600M-to-$3.3B dispersion—and the applicability of stage-progression multipliers in biotech, where value creation can be non-linear and deal-dependent.
